name: Pin&Play
short_description: A ubicomp networking technique that turns everyday surfaces into a two-dimensional power-and-data bus, so objects pinned to a wall or board with pushpin connectors gain network connectivity and power.
long_description: Pin&Play augments familiar surfaces such as walls and notice-boards with layered conductive sheets, turning the surface itself into a shared communication and power medium; objects are connected simply by pinning them with a pushpin-style connector. The authors demonstrated the idea with a smart notice-board, in which pinned notes, controls, and pictures become networked objects without wiring or configuration. It matters as physical AI / interaction design because it reframes attachment-by-pinning - an established everyday gesture - as the act of joining a network, making device ecologies ad hoc, spatial, and tangible rather than hidden in cabling.
ai_note: Pin&Play is a conductive-surface power and networking technique using pushpin connectors; no AI or ML runs in the system.

images.caption: Pin&Play smart notice-board: objects attached to a conductive surface via pushpin connectors receive power and network connectivity (research figure).
images.alt_text: Diagram of a notice-board surface with a pushpin connector attaching an object, illustrating how conductive layers carry power and data through the pin.

sources.title: Pin & Play: Networking Objects through Pins

interactions.title: Pin a note and it joins the network
interactions.description: Press a pushpin-connector through a sheet of paper into the conductive notice-board and the attached object instantly receives power and joins a shared data bus, no wiring or pairing - the everyday act of pinning becomes the act of connecting.

interactions.title: Color-coded priority pins set a reminder
interactions.description: Fasten a document with a red, orange, or green pin and the board sets a reminder for a day, a week, or a month respectively - the choice of pin encodes how urgently you want to be reminded.

interactions.title: An expired note lights its own pin
interactions.description: When a pinned document's reminder time elapses, a small bright blue LED in the pin switches on, standing out clearly among everything else on the board so you can spot what needs attention at a glance.

interactions.title: Rearrange the board freely, nodes come and go
interactions.description: Move a pinned object to another spot or pull it off entirely and the bus treats it as ad-hoc add and removal - the surface itself is the medium, so connectivity follows wherever you stick the pin.

interactions.title: Conductive wallpaper powers wall fixtures
interactions.description: The authors envision the same layered conductive wallpaper carrying power and data to mundane wall-mounted devices - light switches, lamps, and thermostats - so a fixture is installed simply by attaching it to the surface rather than running cable to it.

interactions.title: A studio wall that knows its layout
interactions.description: Pin sketches, swatches, and small displays across a conductive board so each pinned item reports where it sits; rearranging the pins reorganizes a project's digital record - grounded in the system's ad-hoc bus that registers objects as they are pinned and moved.

interactions.title: Errand pins that nag until done
interactions.description: Extend the priority-pin reminder idea to a household chore board: pin a bill or a permission slip with a short-fuse pin and its blue LED lights when it is overdue, clearing only when you unpin the finished task.
